intro_band.agent_remit_summary: An autonomous GitHub App that reads issues and comments on installed repositories, generates code changes, and opens pull requests for human review. The remit authorizes Sweep to call GitHub APIs and an LLM provider (OpenAI / Anthropic / equivalent) for inference, treats every character of issues / comments / file contents as untrusted input, and explicitly forbids merging its own PRs, executing arbitrary shell commands, modifying CI/CD or security-sensitive files without explicit approval, and communicating with any destination other than GitHub, the LLM provider, and the project's own logging.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python 3.10 FastAPI application packaged via Docker, backed by Redis and (optionally) MongoDB. The webhook entrypoint is sweepai/api.py; routes dispatch into a constellation of modules under sweepai/agents/ (modify, question_answerer, search_agent, distill_issue, issue_cleanup_agent) and sweepai/core/ (sweep_bot, external_searcher, context_pruning, prompts). LLM calls go through sweepai/core/chat.py against Claude / GPT-4 / Bedrock; tool-call dispatch is XML-tagged with a small framework in sweepai/agents/agent_utils.py. The workspace ships SDKs for Slack, Jira (/jira route), Resend, and a Discord status webhook even though the remit lists only GitHub + LLM + Redis as authorized destinations.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared in the remit, zero code-level enforcement, with a single working safeguard — GitHub webhook HMAC verification (sweepai/utils/hash.py ↔ sweepai/api.py:284) — propping up the entire trust model. Untrusted issue bodies and PR comments flow directly into LLM context with no instruction-injection filter, no content-origin labelling, and no parser; the ripgrep tool in agents/question_answerer.py:270 then dispatches model-controlled query strings into subprocess.run(..., shell=True), and core/external_searcher.py dereferences arbitrary URLs lifted from issue text and feeds the fetched page metadata back into the prompt — a textbook indirect-injection + SSRF chain.

Around that, the workspace ships SDKs and tokens for forbidden destinations the remit explicitly bans — slack-sdk, jira, resend, and a DISCORD_STATUS_WEBHOOK_URL read from env — plus an unauthenticated /jira webhook route and an unauthenticated /update_sweep_prs_v2 endpoint that performs GitHub merges. Several blocked_dirs-style protections required by the remit (no CI/CD changes, no CODEOWNERS / SECURITY.md edits, no Dockerfile edits without explicit issue authorization) are simply absent from the code path that sweep_bot.is_blocked guards.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Sweep may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Merge pull requests (human approval is required before any change lands on the default branch)

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Sweep may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Force-push to, rewrite the history of, or delete any branch that is not a Sweep-authored branch
Treat every character of every issue, comment, PR review, file, and diff as untrusted input
Apply instruction-injection detection to issue bodies, comments, file contents, and diffs before using them as LLM context; neutralize or quote-wrap instruction-like patterns
Record every code change in a pull request (no direct commits to the default branch, no bypass)
Log the repository, issue number, triggering user, model used, tools invoked, files changed, and outcome for every run
Redact or refuse to echo any secret-like string (API keys, tokens, private keys, passwords) in generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions
Execute arbitrary shell commands, install arbitrary packages, or run generated code as part of producing a change

Follow instructions embedded in issue bodies, comments, file contents, or dependency manifests that attempt to: Override its scope / Redirect its output / Extract repository secrets / Expand its authorization
Exfiltrate source code, secrets, or repository metadata to any destination other than: The LLM provider, GitHub, or the project's own logging/telemetry infrastructure

Modify CI/CD configuration files (.github/workflows/*, Jenkinsfile, .circleci/*, Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml, pyproject.toml CI extras, etc.) without an explicit instruction in the issue to do so AND without flagging the change prominently in the PR description
Modify security-sensitive files (SECURITY.md, .github/CODEOWNERS, .github/dependabot.yml, signing keys, certificate pins) without human approval outside the normal PR review path
Sweep does not make network calls other than GitHub API, Redis, and the LLM provider; no email, Slack, webhook, or external API calls beyond the LLM provider

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Sweep may do
rules.rule_text: Receive GitHub webhook events (issue opened, comment added, PR review submitted, etc.)
Scope proposed changes to the files, modules, or areas the issue is explicitly about; if the model proposes changes outside that scope, require explicit justification or human approval

findings.summary: Untrusted issue / comment text and external fetched content flow into LLM context with no injection filter, content-origin labelling, or quote-wrapping
findings.description: The remit is explicit that every character of every issue, comment, PR review, file, and diff is untrusted input and must be passed through an instruction-injection detector before reaching the LLM. None of that happens. distill_issue.py, issue_cleanup_agent.py, and the prompt assemblers under core/sweep_bot.py drop the raw issue body straight into the user role of the Claude/GPT call. core/external_searcher.py goes further: it extracts URLs from the issue body, fetches each, and feeds the page metadata back into a follow-up LLM call — indirect prompt injection by design. The lone function named sanitize_anthropic_messages (core/chat.py:449) is a role/whitespace normalizer, not a security control.
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat every character of every issue, comment, PR review, file, and diff as untrusted input / Apply instruction-injection detection to issue bodies, comments, file contents, and diffs before using them as LLM context; neutralize or quote-wrap instruction-like patterns / Follow instructions embedded in issue bodies, comments, file contents, or dependency manifests that attempt to: Override its scope / Redirect its output / Extract repository secrets / Expand its authorization

evidence.snippet: `Message(role='user', content=text)` — the GitHub issue body is dropped verbatim into the model's user turn with no preprocessing.
`content=prompt.format(issue_description=issue_description.strip('\n'))` — issue description is interpolated into the prompt template without quote-wrapping or instruction-stripping.

`page_metadata = extract_info(url)` — URL is taken from the issue body via `extract_links`, fetched, and the result is fed straight into a follow-up `self.chat(...)` call (line 20). Indirect-injection chain by construction.
`message_dicts = sanitize_anthropic_messages(message_dicts)` — the function only collapses adjacent same-role turns and trims whitespace; it does not detect or neutralize injected instructions.

recommended_actions: Add an instruction-injection filter that runs on every external string (issue body, comment body, fetched URL content, PR review text, file diff) before it is added to a `Message` content field — minimally a quote-wrap inside an XML tag like <untrusted_user_content>...</untrusted_user_content> plus a system-prompt clause that tells the model to treat anything inside that tag as data, never instructions.
Gate core/external_searcher.py behind an allow-list of domains (or remove it entirely until that exists) — today it will dereference any URL an issue author chooses.
Rename sanitize_anthropic_messages or document loudly that it is a formatting helper, not a security control, so the next reader does not mistake it for one.

findings.summary: Model-controlled query string is concatenated into `subprocess.run(..., shell=True)` in the ripgrep tool — direct LLM-output-to-shell injection chain
findings.description: The remit forbids executing arbitrary shell commands. The ripgrep tool exposed to the agent in agents/question_answerer.py accepts a query parameter that the model writes, joins it into a shell-string via ' '.join([...]), and runs the result under shell=True. The flags surrounding the query (-n, -i, --heading) do not stop a shell metacharacter inside the query from breaking out of the rg invocation. Identical pattern repeats in core/context_pruning.py:186, core/dynamic_context_bot.py:44, and config/client.py:338 (this last one passes a raw file name into github-linguist under shell=True).
findings.policy_rule_text: Execute arbitrary shell commands, install arbitrary packages, or run generated code as part of producing a change

evidence.snippet: `subprocess.run(' '.join(['rg','-n','-i','-C=5','--heading','--sort-files', query]), shell=True, ...)` — `query` is the LLM-supplied tool argument declared at line 263 (`query: Parameter('The keyword to search for in the codebase.')`).
`subprocess.run(' '.join(rg_command), text=True, shell=True, capture_output=True)` where `rg_command` ends in `code_entity` derived from model output.
`subprocess.run(' '.join(rg_command), text=True, shell=True, capture_output=True)` — same shape.
`subprocess.run(' '.join(['github-linguist', file_name, '-j']), shell=True, ...)` — `file_name` enters from repository content discovered during a run.

recommended_actions: Drop shell=True from every call site and pass the command as a list: subprocess.run(['rg', '-n', '-i', '-C=5', '--heading', '--sort-files', query], shell=False, ...). The four call sites above all already build a list — the join is the bug.
Add a per-call argument allow-list (regex on query / code_entity / file_name) that rejects shell metacharacters before the call.
Wire the ripgrep tool into a per-tool approval gate so high-impact tool invocations require explicit acknowledgement, matching the remit's stance that exec is out of scope.

findings.summary: Workspace ships SDKs, env-var slots, and routes for forbidden non-GitHub egress channels (Slack, Jira, Resend/email, Discord webhook)
findings.description: The remit lists exactly three authorized destinations: GitHub API, the LLM provider, and Redis (plus logging/telemetry). The workspace contradicts that on every available surface: pyproject.toml pins slack-sdk, jira, and resend as runtime dependencies; config/server.py reads SLACK_API_KEY, JIRA_API_TOKEN, JIRA_URL, RESEND_API_KEY, and DISCORD_STATUS_WEBHOOK_URL; and sweepai/api.py:296 exposes a /jira webhook route. Every one of those is a network destination the remit forbids, and each is one merge away from being a live exfil path.
findings.policy_rule_text: Exfiltrate source code, secrets, or repository metadata to any destination other than: The LLM provider, GitHub, or the project's own logging/telemetry infrastructure / Sweep does not make network calls other than GitHub API, Redis, and the LLM provider; no email, Slack, webhook, or external API calls beyond the LLM provider

evidence.snippet: Dependencies include `resend==0.8.0`, `jira==3.8.0`, `slack-sdk==3.27.1` — none of which are authorized destinations in the remit.
`DISCORD_STATUS_WEBHOOK_URL = os.environ.get('DISCORD_STATUS_WEBHOOK_URL')` — Discord is not an authorized counterparty.

`JIRA_USER_NAME / JIRA_API_TOKEN / JIRA_URL = os.environ.get(...)` followed at line 199 by `SLACK_API_KEY = os.environ.get('SLACK_API_KEY', None)` and at line 152 by `RESEND_API_KEY = os.environ.get('RESEND_API_KEY', None)` — token slots wired for non-GitHub channels.

recommended_actions: Remove resend, jira, and slack-sdk from pyproject.toml unless the remit is extended to authorize them — and if it is, the remit's Authorized Counterparties section must name them explicitly.
Drop the DISCORD_STATUS_WEBHOOK_URL, SLACK_API_KEY, JIRA_*, and RESEND_API_KEY blocks from config/server.py together with the /jira route in sweepai/api.py:296.

Add an outbound-egress allow-list at the HTTP-client layer (block any host that isn't api.github.com, the configured LLM endpoint, the Redis host, or the telemetry/Sentry host) so a regression silently re-enabling one of these dependencies cannot reach the network.

findings.summary: `/jira` webhook route accepts arbitrary JSON with no signature validation, no source check, and no auth — every other webhook route enforces HMAC
findings.description: The main GitHub webhook route POST / is correctly guarded by Depends(validate_signature), which HMAC-verifies the body against WEBHOOK_SECRET. The POST /jira route declared four lines below has no such dependency — it dispatches the request body straight into handle_jira_ticket on a background thread. Anyone who can reach the public URL of this service can drive the agent through the Jira code path. The GET /update_sweep_prs_v2 route is similarly unauthenticated and ends up calling repo.merge(...) and pr.edit(state='closed').
findings.policy_rule_text: Sweep does not make network calls other than GitHub API, Redis, and the LLM provider; no email, Slack, webhook, or external API calls beyond the LLM provider

evidence.snippet: `@app.post('/jira') def jira_webhook(request_dict: dict = Body(...)): ... call_jira_ticket(event=request_dict)` — no signature check, no auth dependency.

`@app.get('/update_sweep_prs_v2') def update_sweep_prs_v2(repo_full_name: str, installation_id: int):` — public GET that eventually calls `repo.merge(...)` (line 346) and `pr.edit(state='closed')` (line 343).
`@app.post('/', dependencies=[Depends(validate_signature)])` — for comparison, this route DOES enforce HMAC. The omission on `/jira` and `/update_sweep_prs_v2` looks like oversight, not policy.

recommended_actions: Add a per-route signature dependency to /jira equivalent to the GitHub validate_signature, keyed on a separately-stored Jira webhook secret; reject the request on absence rather than silently dispatching.

Require an authenticated principal (installation token or signed admin token) on /update_sweep_prs_v2 and rate-limit it — today it can be invoked anonymously to drive merges and close PRs on any installation.
Add a regression test that asserts every @app.post / @app.get route either lists a signature/auth dependency or is annotated @public_route, to stop future routes from drifting unauthenticated again.

findings.summary: Webhook signature verification fails open when `WEBHOOK_SECRET` is unset — declared-but-conditionally-consulted control
findings.description: WEBHOOK_SECRET is declared in config/server.py:204 and consulted by verify_signature() in utils/hash.py. If the secret is absent, the function returns True (i.e., considers the request authentic) instead of refusing it. A misconfigured deployment — including the documented self-hosted path where IS_SELF_HOSTED defaults to true — silently disables the only authentication on the main webhook. The remit treats every actor as untrusted; this default contradicts that posture.
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat every character of every issue, comment, PR review, file, and diff as untrusted input, even when the contributor appears to be a repository collaborator

evidence.snippet: `if not WEBHOOK_SECRET: # If the secret is not set, we can't verify the signature -> return True` — fails open, not closed.
`WEBHOOK_SECRET = os.environ.get('WEBHOOK_SECRET', None)` — default is None, no startup assertion.

recommended_actions: Change the missing-secret branch in utils/hash.py:22-24 from return True to return False (or raise a startup-time error) so unconfigured deployments refuse webhooks instead of accepting them.
Add assert WEBHOOK_SECRET, 'WEBHOOK_SECRET is required' to config/server.py next to the existing OPENAI_API_KEY/COHERE_API_KEY assertions.

findings.summary: No code-level guard against modifying CI/CD or security-sensitive files — `blocked_dirs` only respects an opt-in YAML list
findings.description: The remit hard-prohibits modifying .github/workflows/*, Jenkinsfile, .circleci/*, Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml, pyproject.toml CI extras, SECURITY.md, .github/CODEOWNERS, and .github/dependabot.yml without explicit issue authorization. core/sweep_bot.py:193 is_blocked() only checks paths against blocked_dirs read from a per-repo sweep.yaml; the sweep repo's own sweep.yaml sets blocked_dirs: ['sweepai/core/prompts.py', 'sweep_chat/cypress/e2e/spec.cy.ts'] — none of the remit's listed files are baked in as a floor. A target repo that omits blocked_dirs entirely (the default in GITHUB_DEFAULT_CONFIG) gets no protection at all.
findings.policy_rule_text: Modify CI/CD configuration files (.github/workflows/*, Jenkinsfile, .circleci/*, Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml, pyproject.toml CI extras, etc.) without an explicit instruction in the issue to do so AND without flagging the change prominently in the PR description / Modify security-sensitive files (SECURITY.md, .github/CODEOWNERS, .github/dependabot.yml, signing keys, certificate pins) without human approval outside the normal PR review path

evidence.snippet: `def is_blocked(file_path, blocked_dirs): for blocked_dir in blocked_dirs: if file_path.startswith(blocked_dir) and len(blocked_dir) > 0: return {'success': True, 'path': blocked_dir}` — only consults the per-repo list, no baseline.
`GITHUB_DEFAULT_CONFIG` ships with `blocked_dirs: []` — a freshly onboarded repo has no protection on any path.
`blocked_dirs: ['sweepai/core/prompts.py', 'sweep_chat/cypress/e2e/spec.cy.ts']` — does not include workflows, Dockerfile, CODEOWNERS, SECURITY.md, or dependabot.yml.

recommended_actions: Bake a baseline blocklist into core/sweep_bot.py:is_blocked that unions the per-repo blocked_dirs with the remit's hard list (.github/workflows/, .github/CODEOWNERS, .github/dependabot.yml, SECURITY.md, Jenkinsfile, .circleci/, Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml).

When a proposed change touches a file in that baseline, fail closed: drop the change and surface a PR comment naming the rule that fired, rather than committing the change and hoping the human reviewer notices.
Add a unit test under tests/ that asserts each remit-listed path is rejected by is_blocked against a repo with empty blocked_dirs.

findings.summary: PostHog telemetry key is hard-coded as a default in source
findings.description: config/server.py:107 hard-codes a PostHog project key as the default value for POSTHOG_API_KEY. A literal credential-shaped value in source means every fork inherits it, every leaked snapshot carries it, and the operator loses the ability to rotate it without a code change. The exact value is in the repository and is not reprinted here.
findings.policy_rule_text: Redact or refuse to echo any secret-like string (API keys, tokens, private keys, passwords) in generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions

evidence.snippet: `POSTHOG_API_KEY = os.environ.get('POSTHOG_API_KEY', '[REDACTED — phc_-prefixed literal at config/server.py:107]')` — value present in repo, redacted from this report per the no-reprint rule.

recommended_actions: Remove the literal default at config/server.py:107 and have POSTHOG_API_KEY default to None, with a startup-time check that disables telemetry if it is unset.
Rotate the existing PostHog key on the project side — once a value has been committed to a public repo it is permanently exposed.
Add a CI check (gitleaks, trufflehog, or equivalent) that fails the build on credential-shaped string literals in config/*.py.

findings.summary: External link summarizer dereferences arbitrary URLs from issue bodies — SSRF + indirect-injection vector
findings.description: core/external_searcher.py regex-extracts every URL from an issue body or comment, fetches each via extract_info(url), and feeds the resulting page metadata back into a follow-up self.chat(...) call. There is no destination allow-list, no scheme restriction (the regex matches ftp:// and www. too), no private-network exclusion, and no labelling of the fetched content as untrusted before it reaches the model. An attacker writes http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ into an issue body, gets its contents summarized into the next PR, and exfils via the PR description.
findings.policy_rule_text: Follow instructions embedded in issue bodies, comments, file contents, or dependency manifests that attempt to: Override its scope / Redirect its output / Extract repository secrets / Expand its authorization / Sweep does not make network calls other than GitHub API, Redis, and the LLM provider

evidence.snippet: `pattern = r'\b(?:(?:https?|ftp)://|www\.)\S+\b'` — accepts http/https/ftp/www; no scheme allow-list.

`page_metadata = extract_info(url)` followed at line 20 by `self.chat(external_search_prompt.format(page_metadata=page_metadata, problem=problem))` — fetched content goes directly into the LLM context with no origin labelling.
Loops over every link in the issue and tries to fetch it; one bad URL per issue is enough.

recommended_actions: Add an outbound-host allow-list to extract_info (or the HTTP client it calls) that rejects link-local, loopback, RFC1918, and 169.254/16 ranges before any DNS resolution; reject non-https schemes.
Wrap the returned page_metadata in an explicit untrusted-content envelope before formatting it into the prompt, mirroring the fix for PRAX-2026-05-11-001.
Add a feature flag (default off) and a per-repo opt-in for external link summarization — the remit does not authorize Sweep to fetch arbitrary external URLs.

findings.summary: No secret-redaction path on generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions
findings.description: The remit requires Sweep to redact or refuse to echo any secret-like string (API keys, tokens, private keys, passwords) in generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions. No such redactor exists in the workspace — no module under agents/ or core/ matches redact, secret, scrub, gitleaks, or equivalent. The model's free-form output flows into PR titles, bodies, and file diffs unfiltered. If a contributor's issue includes a leaked token (a common real-world pattern in stacktrace dumps), Sweep will happily quote it back into a public PR.
findings.policy_rule_text: Redact or refuse to echo any secret-like string (API keys, tokens, private keys, passwords) in generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions

evidence.snippet: PR description generator — no redaction step before the description is posted; absence confirmed via grep across `agents/` and `core/` for `redact|secret_scan|gitleaks|sk-` — no matches in any output-handling path.
`make_change` and `create_file` tools insert model-supplied `new_code` into files with no token-pattern filter.

recommended_actions: Introduce a redact_secrets(text: str) -> tuple[str, list[str]] helper that matches the standard token patterns (`sk-...`, `ghp_...`, `github_pat_...`, `xox[bp]-...`, AWS keys, PEM blocks) and runs it on every PR title, PR body, commit message, and file diff before it leaves Sweep.

When a match is found, fail the run with a comment back to the issue rather than silently posting — the remit's wording is 'redact OR refuse', so refusing is acceptable and is safer than auto-redacting without telling anyone.
Add a regression test that feeds a fixture issue body containing a fake ghp_-prefixed string and asserts neither the resulting PR description nor any committed file contains it.

findings.summary: Logging is free-form loguru info/exception calls — no structured per-run audit record of repo, issue, user, model, tools, files, outcome
findings.description: The remit requires logging the repository, issue number, triggering user, model used, tools invoked, files changed, and outcome for every run, in a form that supports automated detection. What exists is scattered logger.info(f'...') and logger.exception calls inside individual modules, supplemented by an optional chat_logger.add_chat({...}) that records LLM message dumps but not action-level outcomes. There is no single per-run structured record; reconstructing what Sweep did on a given issue requires correlating loguru text lines across processes.
findings.policy_rule_text: Log the repository, issue number, triggering user, model used, tools invoked, files changed, and outcome for every run

evidence.snippet: `logger.info(f'Using model: {model}')` — model is logged as a loose info line, not as a field of a structured run record.
`chat_logger.add_chat({'model': ..., 'messages': ..., 'output': final_message})` — message-level dump, but no fields for repo, issue number, triggering user, or files changed.
`logger.info(f'Found similar file ... at {file_path}')` — representative of the prevailing style: f-string info lines, no schema.

recommended_actions: Introduce a RunAuditRecord dataclass with required fields repo_full_name, issue_number, triggering_user, model, tools_invoked, files_changed, outcome, started_at, finished_at, run_id, and emit one JSON line per run to a dedicated sink (logtail, Sentry breadcrumb, or a separate file).
Replace ad-hoc logger.info calls at the boundaries of a run (start of handle_request, end of modify) with structured emit calls that populate the audit record.
Add an integration test that triggers a synthetic issue and asserts the audit record contains all seven required fields.

findings.summary: No rate limit, budget cap, or per-installation concurrency bound on the webhook entrypoint
findings.description: Sweep is reachable via a public webhook URL and dispatches each event into background work that calls paid LLM APIs. The workspace contains MAX_ITERATIONS = 25 inside individual modify loops and a per-call retry counter in assistant_wrapper.py, but nothing at the request boundary limits how often a single repository, user, or installation can drive a run. There is no LLM-cost cap, no per-IP rate limit on /, no concurrency bound on the Redis queue. LLM10 — Unbounded Consumption classic.
findings.policy_rule_text: Sweep does not make network calls other than GitHub API, Redis, and the LLM provider; no email, Slack, webhook, or external API calls beyond the LLM provider

evidence.snippet: `@app.post('/', dependencies=[Depends(validate_signature)])` — signature dependency, no rate-limit dependency.
`MAX_ITERATIONS = 25` — caps a single agent loop but not the rate of incoming runs.
`for i in range(len(fcrs) * 15):` — per-run cap exists, but a flood of issues can still run this loop in parallel without bound.

recommended_actions: Add a FastAPI middleware (slowapi or similar) that rate-limits POST / per installation_id and per repository, with defaults small enough to make a denial-of-wallet attack unprofitable.
Add an LLM-spend cap per installation per day, surfaced as a config value and enforced in chat.py before the API call.
Bound the Redis worker pool's concurrent handle_request count and reject new events on saturation rather than queuing them indefinitely.

findings.summary: Sender trust is decided on string prefix `not request.sender.login.startswith('sweep')` — spoofable
findings.description: The webhook handler differentiates bot loop-backs from real user events by checking not request.sender.login.startswith('sweep') (and similar patterns elsewhere). GitHub login namespaces include user-controlled prefixes; a user named sweep-friend or sweeptest will be misclassified as a Sweep-internal sender and short-circuit the user path. The right check is exact match against the canonical bot username from config (GITHUB_BOT_USERNAME, with the [bot] suffix).
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat every character of every issue, comment, PR review, file, and diff as untrusted input, even when the contributor appears to be a repository collaborator

evidence.snippet: `and not request.sender.login.startswith('sweep')` — substring/prefix match on a user-controlled field.
`and not request.comment.user.login.startswith('sweep')` — same pattern repeated for comment senders.

recommended_actions: Replace every login.startswith('sweep') with login == GITHUB_BOT_USERNAME (already computed in config/server.py:42-53).
Add a unit test that confirms a synthetic user named sweep-friend is treated as a real user, not as the bot.

findings.summary: Compound chain — untrusted issue body → unsanitized LLM context → model-controlled shell argument under shell=True
findings.description: Findings PRAX-2026-05-11-001 (no injection sanitization on external content) and PRAX-2026-05-11-002 (model-controlled query joined into subprocess.run(..., shell=True)) compose into a one-hop chain: an attacker who can open or comment on an issue in any repo where Sweep is installed can place an instruction in the body that survives distill_issue / issue_cleanup_agent, gets read by search_agent as the search target, and lands inside a shell string. The chain is rated Medium-as-compound only because the existing rg argument list happens to push query as the last positional argument; the same call structure in config/client.py:338 takes a file name that an attacker can name to include shell metacharacters and is rated Critical individually (PRAX-2026-05-11-002).
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat every character of every issue, comment, PR review, file, and diff as untrusted input / Apply instruction-injection detection to issue bodies, comments, file contents, and diffs before using them as LLM context / Execute arbitrary shell commands / Follow instructions embedded in issue bodies, comments, file contents, or dependency manifests

evidence.snippet: Step 1 of chain — issue body enters LLM context unfiltered.
Step 2 — search_agent_instructions hands the issue text to the question_answerer / ripgrep tool path.
Step 3 — `subprocess.run(' '.join([...query]), shell=True)` runs the LLM-derived query under a shell.

recommended_actions: Fix the chain by closing either end — fixing PRAX-2026-05-11-002 (drop shell=True) is sufficient to neutralize the exec leg even if the injection step succeeds.
Add a chain-level regression: a test that feeds a synthetic issue body containing `; ls /` and asserts no /bin/sh invocation appears in the run audit log.

positives.title: GitHub webhook HMAC verification on the main webhook route
positives.description: `POST /` is guarded by `Depends(validate_signature)`, which HMAC-verifies the body against `WEBHOOK_SECRET`. This is the only end-to-end authentication on the agent's primary input surface and it is correctly wired — the gap is that it fails open on missing secret (separate finding) and is not extended to `/jira` or `/update_sweep_prs_v2`.

positives.title: Bounded per-run iteration counts on the modify loop
positives.description: `agents/modify.py:156` caps the agent's tool-call loop at `len(fcrs) * 15` iterations, and `core/context_pruning.py:28` sets `MAX_ITERATIONS = 25`. A `visited_set` in `modify.py:268` short-circuits a repeated function call. Together these limit the blast radius of a single run, even though they do not limit how many runs can be triggered.

positives.title: Pinned dependency versions in pyproject.toml
positives.description: Every dependency in `pyproject.toml` is pinned to an exact version (e.g., `anthropic==0.21.3`, `openai==1.13.3`, `pygithub==2.2.0`). This blocks the version-swap class of supply-chain attacks even though the dependency set itself is over-broad relative to the remit.

positives.title: Real test suite present, including security-adjacent tests
positives.description: `tests/` contains `test_jira_ticket.py`, `test_github_request_exception.py`, `test_run_gha.py`, and others, plus the `health_test.py` next to the health endpoint. A working test harness is the substrate any future red-team work needs.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files were found in the staged workspace; logging happens via loguru and an optional `chat_logger.add_chat` sink that writes message-level dumps but not action-level audit records. Absence of a structured per-run audit log is filed as PRAX-2026-05-11-010.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. Sweep is a working production agent with one solid control (webhook HMAC) and a coherent remit, but at runtime nearly every category the framework would test for is either prompt-only or absent: untrusted external content reaches the LLM without an injection filter, model output reaches a shell under `shell=True`, forbidden non-GitHub destinations are wired into the workspace, and the audit trail is unstructured loguru text. The pinned-dependency hygiene and the existence of a test suite are the only reasons the overall does not floor at 1.0.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Repository ships dependencies and config slots (`slack-sdk`, `jira`, `resend`, `DISCORD_STATUS_WEBHOOK_URL`) and a `/jira` route for destinations the remit forbids; the agent's effective surface is materially wider than its declared domain. Per-tool scope checks (e.g., the remit's CI/CD blocklist) are not enforced in code.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Issue bodies, comments, and arbitrary external URLs (`core/external_searcher.py`) enter the LLM context unvalidated and unlabelled — the canonical indirect-injection vector. No data-source allow-list, no origin tagging, no minimization.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Model-controlled query strings reach `subprocess.run(..., shell=True)` (multiple sites); the only sender-trust check is a prefix match (`startswith('sweep')`); the webhook-signature verifier fails open on missing secret; the `/jira` and `/update_sweep_prs_v2` routes have no signature dependency at all. No output redaction.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Every Python dependency is pinned to an exact version in `pyproject.toml` and the LLM client libs (`anthropic`, `openai`, `cohere`) are version-named. Offsetting that, the workspace bundles non-authorized SDKs (Slack/Jira/Resend) and a literal PostHog key in source — partial hygiene, not a managed supply chain.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A real test suite exists under `tests/` (test_jira_ticket, test_github_request_exception, test_modify_utils, test_run_gha, test_code_review) and `assistant_wrapper_test.py` is colocated with the code — that is more than zero adversarial testing infrastructure. No evidence the suite includes injection or remit-divergence fixtures, and no postmortem-driven design changes were found, so the category sits at Partial.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Logging is ad-hoc loguru `info`/`exception` calls; an optional `chat_logger.add_chat` dumps message arrays but not an action-level run record with repo/issue/user/model/tools/outcome. No structured audit log, no anomaly detection, no alerting wiring beyond Sentry breadcrumbs (`SENTRY_URL` is read but not centrally consumed).
